description: K.7 iteration-3 evals for rls-auth-boundary-auditor. Iter 1 (v1.0) delta=0 (obvious scenarios), iter 2 (v2.0) delta=+0.667 (canonical severity enum discriminator on compound-auth + mat-view + service-role). Iter 3 (v3.0) ships scenarios that require COMBINATORIAL reasoning across multiple objects -- not single-policy or single-table failure modes -- where the gap surfaces only when the auditor follows the data across boundaries that look correct in isolation. (1) cross-policy interaction: two tables A and B each have correct-looking USING clauses, but a JOIN view or RPC that walks both effectively bypasses one of them; (2) conditional-policy split: a policy uses USING with auth.jwt() claim X but WITH CHECK uses claim Y; depending on the auth path (anon -> sign-up vs invited-user -> elevated session) one of X/Y is stale, opening cross-tenant INSERT; (3) nested SECURITY DEFINER + search_path: function f1 calls function f2 both SECURITY DEFINER, f2 has explicit search_path but f1 doesn't; the calling-role's search_path can hijack f2's resolution path via f1. Pass criteria: iter-2 canonical enum baseline + iter-3 cross-object reasoning expressed as concrete follow-the-data trace + remediation that closes the COMBINATORIAL gap, not just the surface gap. Trend target: iter 3 delta >= 0 (non-regressing) to satisfy L2->L3 quantitative criterion.

evals.expected_output: The VIEW v_team_project_summary BYPASSES team_members's tighter SELECT policy through the JOIN. SQL views run with the privileges of the QUERYING role, so the row-level filter on team_members applies: U1 can only see team_members rows where user_id = U1. So U1 sees `team_members(T1, U1)` and NOT `team_members(T1, U2)` or `team_members(T2, U2)`. BUT the JOIN to projects uses team_id from the visible team_members row (T1), and projects' policy permits SELECT on projects where team_id IN (teams U1 is a member of), which includes T1. So U1 sees projects in T1 only, via U1's own membership row. The gap is more subtle: if U1 were also in T2, OR if the projects policy had a cross-team condition, the join would surface team T2's project budgets to U1 because the team_members FILTER does NOT cascade to the projects table's visibility decision through the JOIN. Concrete bypass scenario: a malicious U1 who knows team T2 exists CAN observe T2 project rows IF U1 manages to insert a team_members(T2, U1) row (via a different bypass), because the view-join would then expose T2 projects through U1's new T1+T2 union. Persona must follow-the-data and flag: (a) views inherit policies of underlying tables BUT (b) the join SEMANTICS allow a user to see a row IF EITHER end of the join is permitted, not BOTH. Remediation: rewrite the view as SECURITY INVOKER with explicit join predicate `WHERE tm.user_id = auth.uid()` filtering the team_members side AND `WHERE p.team_id IN (SELECT team_id FROM team_members WHERE user_id = auth.uid())` on projects side -- redundant by design so the policy interaction is explicit, not implicit. OR drop the GRANT and replace with a SECURITY INVOKER function.

evals.prompt:
You are auditing the following Supabase migration. The policy splits USING and WITH CHECK on DIFFERENT JWT claims. Per persona Step 4 (verify tenant scope in USING and WITH CHECK): a split where the two sides reference different claims means the SELECT path and the INSERT/UPDATE path can disagree under specific auth flows.

Migration file:

```sql
-- migrations/20260606150002_invited_member_invoices.sql
CREATE TABLE invoices (
  id uuid PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  org_id uuid NOT NULL,
  created_by uuid NOT NULL,
  amount_cents bigint NOT NULL,
  status text NOT NULL DEFAULT 'draft',
  created_at timestamptz DEFAULT now()
);
ALTER TABLE invoices ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;
ALTER TABLE invoices FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;

-- The SELECT path uses org_id derived from the current JWT's 'active_org_id' claim,
-- which is set when the user explicitly switches their active org in the UI.
CREATE POLICY invoices_select_active_org ON invoices
  FOR SELECT USING (org_id = (auth.jwt() ->> 'active_org_id')::uuid);

-- The INSERT path uses created_by = auth.uid(), no org_id check at all.
CREATE POLICY invoices_insert_self ON invoices
  FOR INSERT WITH CHECK (created_by = auth.uid());

-- The UPDATE path uses created_by = auth.uid() on USING and on WITH CHECK.
CREATE POLICY invoices_update_self ON invoices
  FOR UPDATE USING (created_by = auth.uid()) WITH CHECK (created_by = auth.uid());
```

Auth model: Supabase auth.uid() + a custom JWT claim 'active_org_id' set when a user switches orgs via a custom Edge Function that re-issues the session.

Concrete scenario: user U1 is a member of org A (default) AND org B. U1's current JWT has `active_org_id = A`. U1 INSERTs an invoice with `(org_id = B, created_by = U1, amount_cents = 5000000, status = 'draft')`. What happens? Can U1 do this? After the INSERT, can U1 SELECT this invoice back?

Produce: (1) per-policy posture; (2) gap citing the USING vs WITH CHECK claim split as the failure mode; (3) follow-the-data tracing both SELECT and INSERT paths; (4) migration-shaped remediation closing the split.
evals.expected_output: The INSERT policy `invoices_insert_self` checks ONLY `created_by = auth.uid()`. U1 CAN INSERT an invoice with `org_id = B` even though U1's active_org_id is A and even if U1 is NOT a member of org B (the policy does not verify membership). After INSERT, U1 CANNOT SELECT this invoice back because the SELECT policy requires `org_id = active_org_id (A)`. The row is orphaned: it exists, U1 created it, but U1 cannot read it. Worse: any OTHER user U2 whose active_org_id is B (and who is a member of org B) WILL see this invoice as if it were created by an unrelated U1 -- writer-vs-reader divergence that creates phantom records visible across tenants. Even worse: if U1 later switches active_org_id to B (re-issues JWT), U1 will see this invoice. This is a P1 cross-tenant write leak masked by a SELECT denial. Remediation: align USING and WITH CHECK -- add membership check to INSERT and UPDATE policies, AND verify active_org_id MATCHES org_id at write time (closes the case where U1 with active_org_id=A inserts org_id=B). Concrete SQL: `CREATE POLICY invoices_insert_member ON invoices FOR INSERT WITH CHECK (created_by = auth.uid() AND org_id = (auth.jwt() ->> 'active_org_id')::uuid AND org_id IN (SELECT org_id FROM org_members WHERE user_id = auth.uid()));` plus equivalent on UPDATE.

evals.prompt:
You are auditing the following Supabase migration. TWO SECURITY DEFINER functions chain: f1 calls f2. f2 has an explicit `SET search_path` but f1 does not. The CALLING role's search_path leaks through f1 into f2's resolution context, opening search_path-hijacking against tables f2 references unqualified.

Migration file:

```sql
-- migrations/20260606150003_audit_log_chain.sql
CREATE TABLE audit_log (
  id bigserial PRIMARY KEY,
  actor_id uuid NOT NULL,
  action text NOT NULL,
  payload jsonb,
  created_at timestamptz DEFAULT now()
);
ALTER TABLE audit_log ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;
ALTER TABLE audit_log FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;
CREATE POLICY audit_log_select_own ON audit_log
  FOR SELECT USING (actor_id = auth.uid());

-- Inner function: writes to audit_log table referenced unqualified.
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION log_action(p_action text, p_payload jsonb)
RETURNS void
LANGUAGE plpgsql
SECURITY DEFINER
SET search_path = pg_catalog, public
AS $$
BEGIN
  INSERT INTO audit_log (actor_id, action, payload) VALUES (auth.uid(), p_action, p_payload);
END;
$$;

-- Outer function: invokes log_action; calls auth.uid() unqualified; NO explicit search_path.
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION record_user_event(p_event text, p_details jsonb)
RETURNS void
LANGUAGE plpgsql
SECURITY DEFINER
AS $$
BEGIN
  PERFORM log_action(p_event, p_details);
END;
$$;

GRANT EXECUTE ON FUNCTION record_user_event TO authenticated;
```

Auth model: auth.uid(). The outer function is the one exposed via GRANT. record_user_event runs with the OWNER's privileges (postgres role) due to SECURITY DEFINER.

Concrete attack scenario: a hostile authenticated user U_evil creates a schema `evil_schema` they OWN, inside which they create `CREATE FUNCTION evil_schema.uid() RETURNS uuid LANGUAGE sql AS 'SELECT '00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001'::uuid';`. Then U_evil sets their session `SET search_path = evil_schema, public;` and calls `SELECT record_user_event('test', '{}');`. What happens? Can U_evil cause record_user_event's `log_action` invocation to insert audit_log rows attributed to a different actor_id?

Produce: (1) function-by-function audit including SET search_path declarations; (2) gap citing search_path hijacking via the nested SECURITY DEFINER chain; (3) follow-the-data trace showing how the calling role's search_path leaks; (4) migration-shaped remediation.
evals.expected_output: The GAP is in `record_user_event`: NO explicit `SET search_path`. When U_evil calls this function with their poisoned search_path (`evil_schema, public`), record_user_event runs as DEFINER (postgres role) but resolves unqualified names against U_evil's current search_path. The auth.uid() call inside record_user_event's body resolves to `evil_schema.uid` (not `auth.uid`) which returns the spoofed UUID. Then PERFORM log_action is called -- log_action HAS `SET search_path = pg_catalog, public` so INSIDE log_action's body, auth.uid() resolves correctly. BUT the spoofed UUID has already been computed before the inner SET takes effect, depending on argument evaluation order. More importantly: if record_user_event's body had used auth.uid() directly (rather than passing through), the spoof would land. The vulnerable surface is the entire body of record_user_event before any SET search_path inside it. Persona must flag: every SECURITY DEFINER function MUST have its own SET search_path = pg_catalog, public at the top of the body, regardless of whether inner functions also have one (defense in depth; the inner SET does NOT propagate up the call stack). Remediation: add `SET search_path = pg_catalog, public` to record_user_event's body. ALSO recommend qualifying all references inside SECURITY DEFINER bodies (e.g. `auth.uid()` not `uid()`, `public.audit_log` not `audit_log`).

should_trigger: We're adding a `documents` table to our Supabase project that's scoped per-org. Here's the new migration with the RLS policies. Can you check the tenant isolation before I deploy?
DECISIONS.md says the app is multi-tenant on Supabase but we never locked an RLS policy for the new workspaces table. Review the policy DDL for isolation gaps.

Before I open the PR: this migration in supabase/migrations/ adds a `team_invoices` table with a `CREATE POLICY ... USING (org_id = auth.jwt() ->> 'org_id')`. Does the INSERT path leak across tenants?

I added a SECURITY DEFINER function `record_event()` that writes to an audit_log table, plus a join table linking users to projects. Both are in the new sql/ migration. Are the policies airtight on the join and the function?
Our slice acceptance adds three Supabase tables (orders, order_items, order_audit) but I only wrote a SELECT policy on orders. Audit whether the child tables can leak rows to another tenant.

Something feels off about this policy: I enabled RLS on the `subscriptions` table but the table owner (the migration runner) still seems to see every row regardless of org. Can you look at the DDL before this ships?
should_not_trigger: This migration does `ALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMN status text NOT NULL` on a 40M-row table. Will it hold a lock and take down checkout when it deploys?
We're renaming `customer_name` to `full_name` and dropping the old column in the same migration. Is this safe to apply against production or does it need a two-phase deploy?
I added a new public REST endpoint that takes a user-supplied ID and returns the record. Trace the auth flow and run the OWASP checklist on the handler code.
This diff decrypts SSN and bank account fields and returns them in the admin dashboard response. Check the PII handling and whether anything sensitive leaks to the client.
The migration adds a foreign key on a large table with a bare `ADD CONSTRAINT`. Should this be split into NOT VALID then VALIDATE so it doesn't lock the table?
Run a secret scan and dependency audit reminder on this branch before I merge, and confirm the new external webhook integration is using HTTPS.
